title: Immunoinformatics design of multi-epitope vaccine candidates against "ESKAPE" pathogens as nosocomial infection.
authors: Moghri, Seyed Amir Hossein Mohammadzadeh Hosseini
author_string: Khalaj, Ayda, Moghri, Seyed Amir Hossein Mohammadzadeh Hosseini
abstract: Nowadays, the ESKAPE pathogens-Enterococcus faecium, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Enterobacter spp.- have been identified as emerging threats to public health. Moreover, these groups of pathogens can lead to nosocomial infections due to critical multidrug-resistant, underscoring that effective therapies are urgently needed. In this research, we focused on developing a novel multi-epitope peptide vaccine against ESKAPE pathogens. Hence, we narrowed down 13 antigens from assembly proteins of ESKAPE pathogens due to the high pathogenicity and surface exposure. Then, of these antigens, we filtered the top 36 epitopes based on their elevated antigenicity, and well-conserved sequence and they have been investigated in terms of appropriate attributes such as immunogenicity, MHC binding affinity (nM), allergenicity and toxicity. Through utilizing systematic immunoinformatics and structural vaccinology strategies, we constructed a multi-epitope vaccine consisting of linear B-cell epitopes, CTL epitopes, and HTL epitopes from these significant epitopes. Moreover, the PADRE peptide applied as an adjuvant to boost the immunogenicity of the vaccine. Designed vaccine fulfilled broad population coverage about 94.1 % globally along with appropriate physicochemical characteristics of multi-epitopes vaccine was reported like achievement of higher expression in the Escherichia coli system (CAI = 0.97, and GC contents = 51 %), and secondary and tertiary structures of the vaccine construct were evaluated subsequently. Molecular docking (docking score = -1325.3 kcal/mol) and dynamic simulations (eigen-score = 6.3 × 10<sup>-6</sup>) were applied to appraise the stability of the vaccine-TLR-2 docked structure, which showed that the vaccine has a high affinity for the TLR-2 receptor which is stable. The immune simulation response revealed that both B and T cells had raised responses (up to 700 and 13,000 cells/mm<sup>3</sup>, respectively) to the vaccination peptide. The outcomes demonstrated that the designed multi-epitope vaccine induced robust IgG + IgM responses peaking at ∼800,000 AU/ml and remarkably induced elevated IFN-γ secretion levels (∼500,000 ng/mL) after three doses of injections, suggesting a balanced Th1/Th2 response. Eventually, the in-depth in silico analysis revealed that the suggested multi-epitope vaccine exhibits a robust immune response from both B and T-cells against ESKAPE infection; however, in vitro and in vivo experimentations are highly crucial to validate the protective immunological proficiency of the vaccine.
